title: The Baby-Making Contest That Divided Canada During the Great Depression
summary:
What if someone offered a huge prize to the mom who had the most babies? That sounds crazy, but it actually happened in Canada during the Great Depression!

A rich lawyer named Charles Vance Millar died in 1926. He was known as a prankster (someone who plays tricks). In his will, he left a strange contest: the Toronto mother who gave birth to the most children in ten years would win a big part of his fortune. The prize was about 500,000 Canadian dollars back then—worth nearly 9 million Canadian dollars today!

The contest was called the Great Stork Derby. (A stork is a bird that, in stories, brings babies.) It caused a huge media frenzy. Newspapers followed the leading moms closely. Some people thought it was funny, but others were angry. They argued about whether it was moral to have so many children, especially during the Great Depression when many people were poor.

At that time, birth control was illegal in Canada. Large families were a controversial topic. Some people worried that poor or immigrant families were having too many kids. The contest brought these debates into the open.

In the end, four moms tied for first place. They each won a share of the money. The contest showed how desperate people were during the Depression—and how strange a rich person's joke could become.
why_it_matters: This story shows how a rich person's joke can affect real people's lives and reveal deep problems in society.

keywords.explanation: Someone who plays tricks on others.
A legal document that says who gets a person's money and things after they die.
A bird that, in stories, is said to bring babies.
When news outlets give a story a lot of excited attention.
A person who moves to a new country to live there.

questions.question: Who created the Great Stork Derby?
How long did the contest last?
Why were some people angry about the contest?
options: They thought it was immoral to have so many children during hard times.
They thought the prize was too small.
They wanted the contest to be open to men.
They thought it was too easy to win.
questions.correct_answer: They thought it was immoral to have so many children during hard times.

questions.question: What was illegal in Canada at that time?
options: Rich people leaving money in wills

questions.question: How many mothers tied for first place?

background_read: The Great Depression was a time in the 1930s when many people lost their jobs and were poor.
In Canada, some people worried about having too many children because they could not afford them.
The contest brought these worries into the open.
Article_Structure: WHO: Charles Vance Millar (a rich lawyer) and four Toronto mothers
WHAT: A contest where the mother with the most babies won money
WHY: Because Millar's will had a strange clause, and it became a big news story

perspectives.description: Saw the contest as a chance to win money to help her family during hard times.
Thought it was immoral to encourage having many children when many families were poor.
Sees the contest as a reflection of the debates about immigration and eugenics in that era.
